Revealing the effects of methylation on DNA biological functions and the underlying physical mechanisms

Summary: The applicant found that m5C promotes DNA compact packaging; m5C increases DNA duplex stability but hinders DNA annealing; and m6A promotes homologous recombination DNA repair in cells.

In organisms, DNA can be methylated to regulate gene expression, DNA metabolism, and other processes, and this is one of the major mechanisms of epigenetic regulation. In humans, methylation mainly occurs on the C base at CpG sites (5mC). DNA in human sperm is highly methylated and compressed into toroidal structures. In human somatic chromosomes, highly methylated DNA is tightly compacted into transcriptionally repressed heterochromatin (Figure 1A). What is the relationship between methylation and DNA compaction? Because DNA condensation is far from equilibrium, measuring DNA condensation under equilibrium conditions requires overcoming many interfering factors, which has long been a challenge in this field. We took advantage of the difficulty of RNA condensation and constructed RNA-DNA-RNA composite molecules to prevent DNA from being attracted to the surface (Figure 1B). We measured the kinetics of DNA condensation under equilibrium conditions, quantified the driving free energy of DNA condensation (Figure 1C), and found that methylation increases the driving free energy of DNA condensation by ~30 kBT per turn (Figure 1D). The steric hindrance caused by methyl groups strengthens the interactions between histones and other positively charged proteins and methylated DNA, thereby playing roles in chromatin folding, nucleosome stability, long-range genomic interactions, and other processes. This work provides a new perspective and theoretical basis for in-depth studies of human sperm development and the formation of higher-order chromatin structures (Figure 1, JACS 2020, sole corresponding author).

Figure 1. 5mC methylation promotes DNA condensation (JACS 2020, sole corresponding author). (A) Sperm DNA is highly methylated and highly compacted into toroidal structures. Highly methylated DNA in somatic cells is tightly compacted to form transcriptionally repressed heterochromatin. (B) Manipulation of RNA-DNA-RNA composite molecules using the applicant-invented temperature-controlled (0-70 °C) transverse magnetic tweezers. (C) Measurement of the driving free energy of DNA condensation under equilibrium conditions. (D) Quantification of the effect of 5mC methylation in promoting DNA condensation.

5mC methylation can increase DNA duplex stability, making the duplex more difficult to unzip under high temperature or tensile force. However, we found that although 5mC can stabilize the DNA duplex, it paradoxically hinders DNA reannealing, that is, 5mC kinetically impedes DNA re-pairing. All-atom molecular dynamics simulations revealed that the methyl group on 5mC hinders DNA reannealing mainly through a steric hindrance effect. This work expands the understanding of the relationship between epigenetic modifications and higher-order DNA structure/function, shows that 5mC regulates the DNA reannealing rate, and reveals, from a kinetic perspective, why methylation modifications reduce the rates of DNA replication and transcription (Figure 2, Nucleic Acids Res 2023, last corresponding author, representative work).

Figure 2. 5mC methylation stabilizes duplex DNA but paradoxically hinders DNA reannealing (Nucleic Acids Res 2023, last corresponding author). (A) Single-molecule magnetic tweezers experiments showed that although 5mC increases DNA duplex stability, it also paradoxically hinders DNA reannealing. (B) Theoretical simulations revealed that 5mC hinders DNA reannealing mainly through methyl-group steric hindrance.

Double-strand breaks are the most severe form of DNA damage, and homologous recombination is the most important mode of DNA double-strand repair. Combining multiple cellular and molecular experiments, we found that m6A methylation changes the physical properties of DNA, promotes RecA-mediated homologous recombination DNA repair, and shows cross-species conservation (Figure 3, PNAS 2025, last corresponding author, representative work). This work links epigenetics and homologous recombination, two important fields of research, and opens a new direction for subsequent studies in eukaryotes and higher organisms.

Figure 3. m6A methylation changes the physical properties of DNA and promotes homologous recombination (PNAS 2025, last corresponding author).

Methylation is the most important modification of DNA. Taking advantage of the ability of single-molecule experiments to perform quantitative and dynamic studies, the applicant revealed the effects of methylation on DNA structural dynamics, providing a new perspective and theoretical basis for understanding human sperm development, higher-order chromatin structure, replication and transcription regulation, and DNA repair.
